BEARING 315

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest
January, 1995

Lots of Orienteering in '95

1995 promises to be a big year in the Northwest for orienteering; maybe the biggest ever. Thanks to the efforts of Mike Schuh and others, a comprehensive 1995 schedule of events has been put together. This schedule is published later in this issue; I’ll summarize the highlights here.

Five days of “A” level competition will be held in the Northwest this summer. The Western States championships comes back to Washington on August 12-13, in the premier event of the summer. Sammamish has mapped a new area in Cle Elum, bordering on the existing RosElum map, but significantly less steep and more open (see article later this issue.) Those of you who remember Sammamish’s 1992 “A” event at RosElum know how first-rate this will be. The other three “A” days will be Kamloops, B.C., on July 8-11. Immediately following the Western States will the summer’s other big event, the North American Rogaining Championships at Bald Mountain. This 24-hour Rogaine sponsored by CROC and SAMM promises to be as good as the one two years ago.

Bracketing the summer “A” events is a full series of “B” events. Starting in the spring, we have three one-day “B” events at Drunken Charlie Lake, Moran State Park, and Fire Mountain, followed in May with a pair of two-day “B” meets in Cle Elum. The “B” events resume in September in a 1-day “B” at Greendale, followed by the ever-popular Inland Empire Cup and the Washington State Champs back to Cle Elum once more.

For those going further afield, western North America is also host to two 5-day extravaganzas this summer. The Colorado 5-day will include the USOF convention and be on June 24-July 2. Just two weeks later the Canada 5-Day arrives in Alberta and will draw orienteers from all over the world for this major international event.

In addition, you will find that there are three training weekends this spring. Husky O Club is sponsoring one at Cle Elum Ridge and one at Moran State Park. The third, McQueen Lake, is at Kamloops and sponsored by OABC. Participation in the Husky training will emphasize advanced training and techniques. The training exercises are being designed to help orienteers who intend to take part in advanced competitive orienteering, and participants should be in good physical condition. If you are interested, you must register in advance; check with Bill Cusworth (206-322-6693). You must also register for McQueen Lake (which also includes a junior training program), and space may be limited. Kris Dartnell (604-743-9678) can also answer general questions about registration. Each training session will include a challenging B meet which everyone is encouraged to attend.

Washington State Relay Champs

Mark your calendars! The Washington State Relay Championships will be on Sunday, January 22 on a new color map of Magnuson Park in Seattle. This event will be the school league (W.I.O.L.) relay champs, and the top team from each school will score points for the season team standings. It will also be the championship for orienteering clubs in the state. Plaques will be awarded to the top club team and the top W.I.O.L. team.

This will be a three leg relay, so find two friends. To be championship eligible, all three people must be from the same school or club, but mixed teams are also welcome to participate. The courses will be short and sweet with lots of head-to-head action, and the lengths will be 2 to 2.5 km for the first couple legs and about 3 km for the last. Both W.I.O.L. and club teams will mass-start together on the same set of courses, but there will be forked sections of the course. As well as the relay, a normal novice course will be available, as always.

For this event, pre-registration is strongly encouraged; contacting us ahead of time allows us to prepare your team beautiful pre-marked maps and punchcards. If you don't find a team, pre-register anyway and we'll put you on a team on the day of the event. To register, simply call Eric at (206) 634-2279 or 524-3718, preferably by Wednesday the 18th. If I'm not there, leave a message with you and your teammates' names and school or club.

The mass-start will be at 1:30 P.M. with team check-in from 12:30 to 1:00. If you don't pre-register, don't yet have a team, or are a novice, you should show-up at noon. While we'll do our best to accommodate everyone, we can't guarantee top quality maps to folks who don't pre-register (besides novices).

Note: All relay participants will be issued bib numbers. These should be worn prominently on the chest and not underneath clothing (yes—it's happened before, folks!) Also, the ground is very wet in some areas of the park, so dress accordingly and bring at least a change of socks and shoes. A whole change of clothes is recommended.

Directions: From I-5, take the NE 45th ST exit (#169). Go East on NE 45th just over 1 mile to the bottom of the viaduct hill. Continue East to Sand Point Way and follow it Northeast to the park entrance at NE 65th. Turn right into the park and follow the signs. (The park is just over 3 miles from the freeway via the above route and can also be reached from northbound I-5 via the NE 65th exit.)

Sammamish August Events

Western States Championship "A" Event
Bullfrog Flats: August 12-13, 1995
The venue is a large, beautiful expanse of wooded land bordered by the Cle Elum River. The "Brick" and "Roslyn's Cafe" in TV's Northern Exposure are close by. The terrain is very fast, generally flat pine forest, with frequent open areas. Eastern Washington's lack of underbrush prevails. A new IOF-standard map was prepared for this meet. The Sammamish Orienteering Club is preparing a nine-day schedule of outdoor activities to keep you enthused and enthralled. For details, write: Sammamish O' Club, PO Box 3682, Bellevue, WA 98009 USA.

Bald Mountain Rogaine
The North American Rogaining Championships
August 19-20, 1995
12 to 24 hours of navigational challenge for your team in Washington's Cascades. Just one week after the big "A" Meet. Venue of over 100 square kilometers of forested near-wilderness on the eastern slopes of the Cascades near Cliffdell, WA, Highway 410. Lots of other outdoor activities scheduled or available for the whole period August 12 through 20, 1995. Write for details to the: Columbia River O' Club, 3802 122nd Ave., Vancouver, WA 99682, USA.

Spiral Butte Permanent Rogaine
A TRIM, or practice course for rogainers in arguably the most beautiful wilderness area in the Lower 48! For a mere $5.00, which includes postage, you get two copies of a USGS-modified waterproof color map and description sheet for the same area that was the setting for the fictional Spiral Butte Mishap mystery serial in O/NA Magazine. As long as your party size is 12 or less, come enjoy a great event on your own schedule, and at your own pace. Even fun as a kitchen-table game! Order from the Columbia River Orienteering Club while you are registering for these great August events.

From the Tales of Sacajawea

December 12, SACO had its last meet of 1994. Michel set some nice courses, and I could even run on my own map. I definitely had an unfair advantage. Following the meet, SACO had its first annual meeting. The following was decided. The Bylaws were accepted as written and presented to the members before the meeting. The Board Members for 1995 are: Ruth Miller, Michel Billaux, Frank Skorina and Elis Eberlein with Elis as President. One vacancy on the Board to be filled. Volunteers? The club economy is still in the red, and the club decided to register with USOF as an associate club which is cheaper. With the present development this will probably not be possible next year. The present members also decided against committing to organizing a future A-meet until we see that the club is in a solid development with a big enough core of dedicated members. After the meeting, the present members and our guest from Ellensburg ate Swedish Christmas Food and drank Glogg.

I wish all the SACO members a Happy and Fruitful New Orienteering Year, 1995.
— Elis Eberlein, President

Course Labeling Proposal

Mike Schuh

I would like to propose some standard nomenclature for courses at local events. The standard names would be used for all "ordinary" courses, but not for score, memory, or other unusual courses.

THE PROBLEM
At local events, we label our courses with numbers, starting (usually) with 1 for the shortest. Beyond about course 2, there is no similarity between course n at one event and a like numbered course at another. This can be a problem for newcomers — "gosh, last time at Woodland Park I did course 4, and it was OK, so I decided to try course 4 here in St. Edward, and...." In addition, the numbers are not very descriptive of either the technical or physical level of the course. Confusion reigns.

A PROPOSAL
Start with a set of basic course names, and add adjectives to indicate different lengths, increased physical demand, or tougher technical challenge. Length is measured in expected winning time. Physical demand is indicated to be greater if the climb exceeds 5% of the length.

BASIC NAMES
Novice

  • all controls on major features
  • obvious route choices using handrails
  • possible for a novice to walk in 20-30 minutes

Beginner

  • all controls on major features
  • routes primarily along handrails
  • possible for slightly experienced person to walk in about 30 minutes

Intermediate

  • controls just off of linear features, usually on the far side of collecting features
  • multiple routes possible
  • expected winning time of 30 minutes

Advanced

  • controls on point features, often on the near side of catching features
  • routes through the forest; few handrails
  • expected winning time of 45 minutes

LENGTH AND CLIMB MODIFERS

  • Long add 15 minutes
  • Very long add 30 minutes
  • Extra long add 45 minutes
  • Short subtract 15 minutes
  • Steep climb is greater than 5% of length
  • Very steep climb is greater than 10% of length

TECHNICAL MODIFERS
(These are very subjective. Use with deliberation.)

  • Easy
  • Difficult (generally not applied to advanced courses)

USAGE
Combining these terms would be done in the following sequence:
[length][climb][technical][basic name]
A course identified as "Extra long, very steep difficult advanced" would be some 90 minutes of rather demanding orienteering. A "Short novice" course would be something easier, and both descriptions are much more informative than "course 4" and "course 1".

EXAMPLE
From the recent Woodland Park meet:
Course 1: Novice
Course 2: Beginner
Course 3: Easy Intermediate
Course 4: Intermediate
Course 5: (can't be named under this proposal)

COMPARISON

USOF name This proposal
White Novice
Yellow Beginner
Orange Intermediate
Brown Short Advanced
Green Advanced
Red Long advanced
Blue Very long advanced

RATIONALE
Why not use USOF colors? Because novices have no clue what "white" or "blue" mean, and it creates just one more obstacle to their getting active in the sport. Besides, the colors are hardly descriptive, and are limited to just seven possibilities. With the system outlined above, there are many more possible names (180), making it easier to accurately fit a name to a course. Consider Woodland Park, where no course will ever really be more difficult than Orange, leaving just three course names: White, Yellow, and Orange.

Using a standard set of course descriptions, we can make it easier for participants to choose the course that best sets their interests.

Membership Report

Aaron "the Alchemist" Charlop

Happy New Year to everyone. Along with the changing of the calendar on the wall, it is time to tally the membership in the club. So I have been busy counting up the official membership as of Dec. 15th, 1994. The numbers I determine are used for insurance purposes, USOF dues for the club, and for determining the number of votes we have in USOF matters. This also marks the end of my first year as membership coordinator. It has been a good year over all. The total membership has increased this year to 194. This is broken down as 82 individual members and 56 family memberships, family memberships count as 2 in the official tally. We have had 27 individual and 17 new family members join the club in 1994 representing 61 new members, approximately 31% of the total membership. Renewals have also increased this year in part due to the new renewal notice sent out to expiring members. Overall the COC membership has a net increase of 41 members for 1994. This is a significant change since the membership has been approximately 150 for over 10 years. In addition to the regular members we have 13 'Newsletter Only' members and send out 26 exchange/publicity newsletters.

Due to a communications error [I screwed up! – Ed.], my report did not make it into the November newsletter. Therefore the list of new and renewing members is for the period from September 1994 through December 1994. There were 4 new memberships received; Jamie Brightman, Marshall Brown and Pat Vetter, Eric Danielson, and Richard Staudt. Welcome to the club. And thank you to our 12 renewing members Martha Anderson, the Bingham family, Harvey Friedman and Anne York, the Haines family, Kent Jameson, Pat Kennedy, Laurie Kohli, Jonathan Newmark, John H. Nitardy, Brad Robinson, the Ward family, Bob Williamson, and the Woods family. Thank you for your continued support.

On a different note, the phone company is adding a new area code to western Washington. As of January 15th all areas outside of the metropolitan Everett-Seattle-Tacoma corridor will have the new area code 360. Those of you who are in the new area code have probably been advised of the fact. However, since I live in the Seattle I have not yet been informed of the affected areas or prefixes. Therefore, if you live within the new area code I would appreciate a call or e-mail message informing me of the fact so that I may update the database appropriately. My e-mail address is charlop@u.washington.edu and my phone number is (206) 440-1497 between 9:00 AM and 9:00 PM. Thank you for your cooperation.

So How Do Those Rankings Work, Anyway?

Bill Cusworth

It's the end of the year and I thought I would attempt to explain in detail all the factors which go into producing the local rankings which have appeared in most editions of Bearing 315 this year. Since my last article on the rankings a year ago, I have somehow convinced the powers that be (namely James Baker, USOF Vice President for Competition) that I know something about what I am doing and have been appointed the position of USOF Rankings Coordinator. Consequently, I have busy working to finish the 1994 end of the year national rankings as well as the local rankings. Fortunately, I received a very nice program from Wyatt Riley this past summer while he was in the area working for Microsoft.™ Wyatt is from the San Francisco Bay Area and has been calculating orienteering rankings for them for many years. Wyatt wrote the program in Visual Basic™ to run within the Excel™ spreadsheet program as this was the software that he was helping develop at Microsoft.™ I had decided to use Wyatt's methodology when I started ranking local orienteering events about a year ago but having his program to use rather than the terrible one that I wrote myself was a big time saver. I used his program for the rankings published in the last newsletter and have now updated his program to calculate the national rankings for 1994 and I am in the process of working on the new system to be used in 1995. The USOF Board of Directors has yet to finalize its approval of the new system but have basically adopted Wyatt's method with a few modifications. It is my intention to calculate the local rankings using the same methodology as the national rankings.

So, who gets a ranking? To be ranked you need to compete in at least 4 races on an advanced level course. At multi-race events, each race is considered separate and no total times are used. Special events such as score-O, relays, canoe-O, and rogaines are not used but mass start events are (although there weren't any of those in 1994). Previously, I had been using only the longest course at each event due mostly to workload considerations but now I include all advanced courses, which is usually Course 3 and up for most meets. For each race, you are given ranking points that are inversely proportional to your time. For example, if you took twice as long as someone else, you would receive half of their points for that race. These ranking points are averaged for the best 75% of your races. If 75% of your races produces an a non-integer, a weighted average is taken. For example, if you had 10 races, 75% or 7.5 of them would get averaged. To calculate the weighted average for this example, you would add the 7 highest ranking points plus 0.5 times the 8th highest ranking points and then divide by 7.5. The rankings are scaled such that the average of the ranking points for the top 3 ranked competitors equals 100.

So, how are the rankings calculated? The main problem with ranking races is that there is a different competitive field at each event. It is therefore not accurate to just give some arbitrary points to the winner. It is also not possible to score a course based on its length, climb or perceived difficulty. Therefore, a method is needed to scale each course that is dependent on the actual times recorded by the competitors. This is done by first multiplying each competitor's time by his average ranking points. The product, called the Personal Gnarliness Value (PGV) (this terminology was coined by J-J Cote), should be constant for everyone on the course if they had a perfectly average race since a competitor's ranking points are inversely proportional to their times. However, if you run slower than usual, this number will be high, and if you run faster, this number will be low. These PGV's are then "averaged" together to give an overall Course Gnarliness Value (GV). To determine a competitor's rank points for a race, you simply divide the GV by the competitor's time. The reason I put averaged in quotes is that there are several different ways of determining the central tendency of a group of PGV's. A straight average is not taken since it is influenced too much by very large PGV's corresponding to people who ran very slow. It is more common to run much slower than usual than it is to run much faster so the distribution of PGV's for a race is skewed with a peak closer to the lower PGV's. To get a GV which best represents the peak of the distribution, the 2 most common methods are to use the harmonic mean or take a PGV at some percentile. The harmonic mean is used by Wyatt Riley and J-J Cote uses the PGV at the 40th percentile. I have been using the harmonic mean but will probably switch to the percentile method if it becomes the standard for national rankings. The harmonic mean is the inverse of the average of the inverses. For example the mean of 1 and 2 is 1.5 but the harmonic mean is 1/ [(1/1 + 1/2) /2] = 4/3 = 1.33. The harmonic mean is essentially a weighted average where the lowest numbers get the highest weight and the largest numbers get the lowest weight.

In order to scale courses using this method, previous rankings for competitor's can be used or a better way is to use iteration. First, you start with a fake set of ranking points such as 50 for everyone and then calculate PGV's and GV's for each course which then produces ranking points for each competitor. These calculated ranking points are then used to calculate a new set of PGV's and GV's and the whole process is iterated until everyone's ranking points don't change much (< .01 between iterations).

DNF's, MSP's, and other forms of disqualification are hard to deal with. I used to give 0 ranking points and for the last rankings (Nov. 94) I used Wyatt's method of assigning a time equal to double the slowest competitor on the course. I have now switched to the USOF method of assigning a time equal to the course time limit (usually 3 hours) + 20 minutes which is usually 200 min.

The last step of the rankings is to convert ranking points back to a representative time to give a "par time". This is done by assigning the 100 pt. runner an arbitrary time that is normal for the course being ranked and then assigning all other rank points times based on this ratio. For the local rankings, I have using 40 min. as the arbitrary time since it is roughly the average winning time for the courses being ranked.

If you think this is all very complicated, you're probably right. If anyone has any questions or would like to know their points at individual events, you can contact me by phone at 322-6693 or by e-mail (bill3@u.washington.edu).

WE NEED Help!

Although we have a full schedule planned for the year ahead, we can't pull it off unless we have help from you, our members. Every event needs some volunteers (sometimes a few, sometimes many) to put in the few hours needed to make the event a success. In order for the club to grow and flourish, these jobs need to be spread around among all the members; if just a few people are working at every event, they quickly become burned out. If everyone helps out one or two events a year, though, we can put on every more great orienteering events.

There are basically three different jobs that we need for each event. They are:
Meet Director. This is our most pressing need. The meet director is the person who handles the organizational aspects of the meet. For a local C event, this involves an hour or two of work in the few weeks before the event: talking to the course setter and meet staff, picking up club equipment and making sure all needed supplies are there, in a few cases talking with land owners or the rangers (for park meets, this generally is already taken care of). During the event, you'll make sure that your meet staff is in the right places, and keep everything running smoothly. After the event, make sure all the equipment is returned intact, fill out the after event form, and submit the proceeds to the treasurer. Although it may sound a bit intimidating, it really isn't more than a few hours of work beyond actually attending the meet. You don't need any experience; the club officers will be happy to give you advice and help, and there is a carefully written guide describing what you need to do.
Course Setter. This is the most time consuming job involved with putting on an event, but many think it is also the most fun. The course setter designs the courses, goes out well in advance (usually a week to a month) of the event and marks each control point. On the day (or day before) the event, he/she (possibly with a helper) places the actual control markers. The course setter also produces the master maps and course descriptions. Unlike the other jobs, being a course setter does require some significant orienteering experience. But it is a lot of fun! If you'd like to try your hand at course setting, we'd be happy to teach you how.
Day Meet Staff. These are the people who do the timing, registration, and so forth on the day of the event. The meet director will tell you exactly what needs to be done. If you're going to take part in the event also, you'll typically do that at the very beginning (before the regular competition) or at the end. Absolutely no experience is required, and it's very easy, but these people are essential!

So, now that you what's involved, why not scan over the list of events this year and find one or two nearby that you can help with as meet staff, or even better, as meet director or course setter. Then call Bill Cusworth (322-6693) for Cascade events, or the listed club president or event contact for other events. If you're still unsure of what's involved, call us anyway, and we'll explain it to you. For future events, give us your name now, and we'll call you as the event approaches.
Thanks for your help!

Submission Guidelines

Bearing 315 welcomes submissions of articles, letters, or other material about orienteering. If you would like your submission returned, please include a self-addressed envelope. The submission deadline is the 23rd of each even month for the issue to be mailed on the following 1st. Submissions should be mailed to:

Bearing 315
c/o Peter Golde
14422 NE 42nd Pl. #801
Bellevue, WA 98007

If possible, please submit on an IBM or Macintosh diskette, or submit via electronic mail to petergo@microsoft.com. If you use a word processor, please submit in Microsoft Word (Mac, DOS, or Windows), WordPerfect, or RTF format; plain text format is also an option. Meet directors, please send meet results to Debbie Newell for compilation.

Field Notes

  • Debbie Newell is taking orders for anyone who would like to purchase a Washington State O' suit. They have a burgundy body, black yoke, and attractive teal stripes on pants and top. Just look around, you will probably see someone wearing one. They are great for traveling to meets outside Washington or for local meets to help beat the salal. Total cost for each suite will be approximately $40 and requires a $20 deposit. Call Debbie at 488-7471.

  • COC, in its continuing effort to make a meaningful contribution in support of National Trails Day and Month, has again set up a series of trail projects at St. Edward State Park, the site of the Bog Slog Auld Lang Syne. The main project is truly a flagship one as we will be rerouting approximately 1800 feet of trail near the top of the park—which thus will be visible and usable by most park visitors—that will eliminate the current steps. We will be hosting this project in conjunction with Volunteers for Outdoor Washington. Since this project is near the parking lot area almost, thus eliminating the 15 minute walk to the site we had last year, we have decided to hold our work parties on each of the Tuesday nights in June from 5pm to 8pm. (If you can't show up on time, don't worry, you can just about see us working from the parking lot.) Rick Hood, 367-2606, will be coordinating the project and hopes everyone will consider coming out for one or more sessions and helping us further our rapport with Washington State Parks. (All tools are provided, just wear work clothes.)

  • Sammamish continues to assault the OCAD learning curve. I am still deep in the bowels of SAMM's new map: Boeing Creek. With deadlines quickly approaching we have changed course and will not be offset printing the map. Besides time constraints we would like to gang print it with other SAMM maps that have yet to be completely updated and redrafted. I have been told however that Mark Howlett has completed Pioneer Park Drafting from the recent field update. The program is simple enough, although not completely intuitive. Input in the form of scanning and output (printing) seem to be the biggest hassles from my point of view. I am still trying to figure out how one would scan in a A-meet sized map. Wanting to use AutoCad commands one is used to also can lead to frustration. Well back to the map. — Dave Lilly

  • Congratulations to Peter Golde, who was recognized as Cascade Orienteering Club's "President's Service Award" Recipient for 1994. This award is given to an individual who has made a significant contribution to the club over the past few years and has further distinguished him- or herself by making a monumental contribution for the past year. In 1994, Peter designed and set courses for our A Meet and published the Bearing 315. In recent prior years, Peter has served as COC president, vice-president, Bearing 315 editor, and frequent course setter/meet director. I know I speak for all of us when I say the honor is well deserved.

  • The Husky Orienteering and Running Club is doing some serious training this year. They meet every weekday at 5:30 in front of the IMA for running or other orienteering training. They are working towards this years Intercollegiate Champs and US Team Trials. If you'd like more information, call Bill Cusworth.

  • Our wondrous outreach coordinator, Rick Hood, made connections with the Puget Sound Mycological Society (mushroom club) and arranged for a talk to be presented and an honorarium to be provided. As all might have guessed, the talk was to be on 'finding your way in the woods' as obviously that is what we orienteers are reasonably experienced at. The Cascade member with both the interest and presentation ability turned out to be Anne York. Anne presented a talk at a regular PSMS club meeting for a mixed age group of mostly adults entitled "An Introduction to Orienteering for Mushroomers or How not to get lost while looking at the ground". She prepared overhead transparencies of sample maps, a compass, what skills one could use relating the two, etc. that were quite helpful. One of these overheads was

    How orienteering skills can help you:

    1. Basic skill to get back to the car
    2. Go further into the forest with confidence.
    3. Locate new places for mushrooming
    4. Relocate a good patch.
    5. Relocate an injured partner.

    She kept the talk humorous without losing the interest of the audience when explaining the serious methods orienteers use that could be applied by mushroomers. Naturally, she also tried to convince them to practice or learn their newly discovered techniques by coming to our local O' meets. Not only was she thanked for the presentation, but several folks gave her their name and address to get more info about O' and at least one came to a meet at Bridle Trails the weekend after the talk. PSMS provided an honorarium of $50 which Anne generously donated to Cascade O. C.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United States Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event Fees

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.

Category Fee
Club members (any club) $3.00
Non-club members $4.00
Junior (< 18, except WIOL) $2.00
WIOL (school league) participants $3.00
First time orienteering participants $2.00
Families $1/person after first two

Woodland Park

WIOL Preseason
December 10, 1994
Cascade Orienteering Club

Courses: Mike Schuh

For many years I had joked that those local orienteers who might be called Woodland Park veterans—folks who have orienteered there many, many times—could complete a course without a map, or at least without the control locations marked on a map. Features like "the stone wall" and "the stump" seemed to get used just about every time we have an event there. So, I thought, why not set a course where the participants get a control card and a description sheet and nothing else?
Nine brave souls tried the course, and all but relative new comer Nick Bone found all of the controls (he didn't figure out the above mentioned wall). Of those who took part, Debbie was probably the one with the most experience in the park and Sandy, who placed second, contributed to one of the many re-mapping efforts of the park. Jim had difficulty reading the control code on one marker and so lost a few minutes. Anne was the only one to try the course without a map, which made her (and those watching) wonder why she was taking her compass with her as she started. Everyone seemed to enjoy the challenge. — Mike Schuh

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

block_index.text: The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest January, 1995

1995 promises to be a big year in the Northwest for orienteering; maybe the biggest ever. Thanks to the efforts of Mike Schuh and others, a comprehensive 1995 schedule of events has been put together. This schedule is published later in this issue; I’ll summarize the highlights here.

Five days of “A” level competition will be held in the Northwest this summer. The Western States championships comes back to Washington on August 12-13, in the premier event of the summer. Sammamish has mapped a new area in Cle Elum, bordering on the existing RosElum map, but significantly less steep and more open (see article later this issue.) Those of you who remember Sammamish’s 1992 “A” event at RosElum know how first-rate this will be. The other three “A” days will be Kamloops, B.C., on July 8-11. Immediately following the Western States will the summer’s other big event, the North American Rogaining Championships at Bald Mountain. This 24-hour Rogaine sponsored by CROC and SAMM promises to be as good as the one two years ago.

Bracketing the summer “A” events is a full series of “B” events. Starting in the spring, we have three one-day “B” events at Drunken Charlie Lake, Moran State Park, and Fire Mountain, followed in May with a pair of two-day “B” meets in Cle Elum. The “B” events resume in September in a 1-day “B” at Greendale, followed by the ever-popular Inland Empire Cup and the Washington State Champs back to Cle Elum once more.

For those going further afield, western North America is also host to two 5-day extravaganzas this summer. The Colorado 5-day will include the USOF convention and be on June 24-July 2. Just two weeks later the Canada 5-Day arrives in Alberta and will draw orienteers from all over the world for this major international event.

In addition, you will find that there are three training weekends this spring. Husky O Club is sponsoring one at Cle Elum Ridge and one at Moran State Park. The third, McQueen Lake, is at Kamloops and sponsored by OABC. Participation in the Husky training will emphasize advanced training and techniques. The training exercises are being designed to help orienteers who intend to take part in advanced competitive orienteering, and participants should be in good physical condition. If you are interested, you must register in advance; check with Bill Cusworth (206-322-6693). You must also register for McQueen Lake (which also includes a junior training program), and space may be limited. Kris Dartnell (604-743-9678) can also answer general questions about registration. Each training session will include a challenging B meet which everyone is encouraged to attend.
So How Do Those Rankings Work, Anyway? ..... 4

Mark your calendars! The Washington State Relay Championships will be on Sunday, January 22 on a new color map of Magnuson Park in Seattle. This event will be the school league (W.I.O.L.) relay champs, and the top team from each school will score points for the season team standings. It will also be the championship for orienteering clubs in the state. Plaques will be awarded to the top club team and the top W.I.O.L. team.

This will be a three leg relay, so find two friends. To be championship eligible, all three people must be from the same school or club, but mixed teams are also welcome to participate. The courses will be short and sweet with lots of head-to-head action, and the lengths will be 2 to 2.5 km for the first couple legs and about 3 km for the last. Both W.I.O.L. and club teams will mass-start together on the same set of courses, but there will be forked sections of the course. As well as the relay, a normal novice course will be available, as always.

For this event, pre-registration is strongly encouraged; contacting us ahead of time allows us to prepare your team beautiful pre-marked maps and punchcards. If you don't find a team, pre-register anyway and we'll put you on a team on the day of the event. To register, simply call Eric at (206) 634-2279 or 524-3718, preferably by Wednesday the 18th. I would like to propose some standard nomenclature for courses at local events. The standard names would be used for all "ordinary" courses, but not for score, memory, or other unusual courses.

THE PROBLEM At local events, we label our courses with numbers, starting (usually) with 1 for the shortest. Beyond about course 2, there is no similarity between course n at one event and a like numbered course at another. This can be a problem for newcomers — "gosh, last time at Woodland Park I did course 4, and it was OK, so I decided to try course 4 here in St. Edward, and...." In addition, the numbers are not very descriptive of either the technical or physical level of the course. Confusion reigns.

A PROPOSAL Start with a set of basic course names, and add adjectives to indicate different lengths, increased physical demand, or tougher technical challenge. Length is measured in expected winning time. Physical demand is indicated to be greater if the climb exceeds 5% of the length.
possible for a novice to walk in 20-30 minutes
possible for slightly experienced person to walk in about 30 minutes
controls just off of linear features, usually on the far side of collecting features
controls on point features, often on the near side of catching features
routes through the forest; few handrails
Steep climb is greater than 5% of length
Very steep climb is greater than 10% of length
TECHNICAL MODIFERS (These are very subjective. Use with deliberation.)
Difficult (generally not applied to advanced courses)

USAGE Combining these terms would be done in the following sequence: [length][climb][technical][basic name] A course identified as "Extra long, very steep difficult advanced" would be some 90 minutes of rather demanding orienteering. A "Short novice" course would be something easier, and both descriptions are much more informative than "course 4" and "course 1".
EXAMPLE From the recent Woodland Park meet: Course 1: Novice Course 2: Beginner Course 3: Easy Intermediate Course 4: Intermediate Course 5: (can't be named under this proposal)

RATIONALE Why not use USOF colors? Because novices have no clue what "white" or "blue" mean, and it creates just one more obstacle to their getting active in the sport. Besides, the colors are hardly descriptive, and are limited to just seven possibilities. With the system outlined above, there are many more possible names (180), making it easier to accurately fit a name to a course. Consider Woodland Park, where no course will ever really be more difficult than Orange, leaving just three course names: White, Yellow, and Orange.
Using a standard set of course descriptions, we can make it easier for participants to choose the course that best sets their interests.

So How Do Those Rankings Work, Anyway?

It's the end of the year and I thought I would attempt to explain in detail all the factors which go into producing the local rankings which have appeared in most editions of Bearing 315 this year. Since my last article on the rankings a year ago, I have somehow convinced the powers that be (namely James Baker, USOF Vice President for Competition) that I know something about what I am doing and have been appointed the position of USOF Rankings Coordinator. Consequently, I have busy working to finish the 1994 end of the year national rankings as well as the local rankings. Fortunately, I received a very nice program from Wyatt Riley this past summer while he was in the area working for Microsoft.™ Wyatt is from the San Francisco Bay Area and has been calculating orienteering rankings for them for many years. Wyatt wrote the program in Visual Basic™ to run within the Excel™ spreadsheet program as this was the software that he was helping develop at Microsoft.™ I had decided to use Wyatt's methodology when I started ranking local orienteering events about a year ago but having his program to use rather than the terrible one that I wrote myself was a big time saver. I used his program for the rankings published in the last newsletter and have now updated his program to calculate the national rankings for 1994 and I am in the process of working on the new system to be used in 1995. The USOF Board of Directors has yet to finalize its approval of the new system but have basically adopted Wyatt's method with a few modifications. It is my intention to calculate the local rankings using the same methodology as the national rankings.

So, who gets a ranking? To be ranked you need to compete in at least 4 races on an advanced level course. At multi-race events, each race is considered separate and no total times are used. Special events such as score-O, relays, canoe-O, and rogaines are not used but mass start events are (although there weren't any of those in 1994). Previously, I had been using only the longest course at each event due mostly to workload considerations but now I include all advanced courses, which is usually Course 3 and up for most meets. For each race, you are given ranking points that are inversely proportional to your time. For example, if you took twice as long as someone else, you would receive half of their points for that race. These ranking points are averaged for the best 75% of your races. If 75% of your races produces an a non-integer, a weighted average is taken. For example, if you had 10 races, 75% or 7.5 of them would get averaged. To calculate the weighted average for this example, you would add the 7 highest ranking points plus 0.5 times the 8th highest ranking points and then divide by 7.5. The rankings are scaled such that the average of the ranking points for the top 3 ranked competitors equals 100.

So, how are the rankings calculated? The main problem with ranking races is that there is a different competitive field at each event. It is therefore not accurate to just give some arbitrary points to the winner. It is also not possible to score a course based on its length, climb or perceived difficulty. Therefore, a method is needed to scale each course that is dependent on the actual times recorded by the competitors. This is done by first multiplying each competitor's time by his average ranking points. The product, called the Personal Gnarliness Value (PGV) (this terminology was coined by J-J Cote), should be constant for everyone on the course if they had a perfectly average race since a competitor's ranking points are inversely proportional to their times. However, if you run slower than usual, this number will be high, and if you run faster, this number will be low. These PGV's are then "averaged" together to give an overall Course Gnarliness Value (GV). To determine a competitor's rank points for a race, you simply divide the GV by the competitor's time. The reason I put averaged in quotes is that there are several different ways of determining the central tendency of a group of PGV's. A straight average is not taken since it is influenced too much by very large PGV's corresponding to people who ran very slow. It is more common to run much slower than usual than it is to run much faster so the distribution of PGV's for a race is skewed with a peak closer to the lower PGV's. To get a GV which best represents the peak of the distribution, the 2 most common methods are to use the harmonic mean or take a PGV at some percentile. The harmonic mean is used by Wyatt Riley and J-J Cote uses the PGV at the 40th percentile. I have been using the harmonic mean but will probably switch to the percentile method if it becomes the standard for national rankings. The harmonic mean is the inverse of the average of the inverses. For example the mean of 1 and 2 is 1.5 but the harmonic mean is 1/ [(1/1 + 1/2) /2] = 4/3 = 1.33. The harmonic mean is essentially a weighted average where the lowest numbers get the highest weight and the largest numbers get the lowest weight.

In order to scale courses using this method, previous rankings for competitor's can be used or a better way is to use iteration. First, you start with a fake set of ranking points such as 50 for everyone and then calculate PGV's and GV's for each course which then produces ranking points for each competitor. These calculated ranking points are then used to calculate a new set of PGV's and GV's and the whole process is iterated until everyone's ranking points don't change much (< .01 between iterations).

DNF's, MSP's, and other forms of disqualification are hard to deal with. I used to give 0 ranking points and for the last rankings (Nov. 94) I used Wyatt's method of assigning a time equal to double the slowest competitor on the course. I have now switched to the USOF method of assigning a time equal to the course time limit (usually 3 hours) + 20 minutes which is usually 200 min.

The last step of the rankings is to convert ranking points back to a representative time to give a "par time". This is done by assigning the 100 pt. runner an arbitrary time that is normal for the course being ranked and then assigning all other rank points times based on this ratio. For the local rankings, I have using 40 min. as the arbitrary time since it is roughly the average winning time for the courses being ranked.

If you think this is all very complicated, you're probably right. If anyone has any questions or would like to know their points at individual events, you can contact me by phone at 322-6693 or by e-mail (bill3@u.washington.edu).

Although we have a full schedule planned for the year ahead, we can't pull it off unless we have help from you, our members. Every event needs some volunteers (sometimes a few, sometimes many) to put in the few hours needed to make the event a success. In order for the club to grow and flourish, these jobs need to be spread around among all the members; if just a few people are working at every event, they quickly become burned out. If everyone helps out one or two events a year, though, we can put on every more great orienteering events.

There are basically three different jobs that we need for each event. They are: Meet Director. This is our most pressing need. The meet director is the person who handles the organizational aspects of the meet. For a local C event, this involves an hour or two of work in the few weeks before the event: talking to the course setter and meet staff, picking up club equipment and making sure all needed supplies are there, in a few cases talking with land owners or the rangers (for park meets, this generally is already taken care of). During the event, you'll make sure that your meet staff is in the right places, and keep everything running smoothly. After the event, make sure all the equipment is returned intact, fill out the after event form, and submit the proceeds to the treasurer. Although it may sound a bit intimidating, it really isn't more than a few hours of work beyond actually attending the meet. You don't need any experience; the club officers will be happy to give you advice and help, and there is a carefully written guide describing what you need to do. Course Setter. This is the most time consuming job involved with putting on an event, but many think it is also the most fun. The course setter designs the courses, goes out well in advance (usually a week to a month) of the event and marks each control point. On the day (or day before) the event, he/she (possibly with a helper) places the actual control markers. The course setter also produces the master maps and course descriptions. Unlike the other jobs, being a course setter does require some significant orienteering experience. But it is a lot of fun! If you'd like to try your hand at course setting, we'd be happy to teach you how. Day Meet Staff. These are the people who do the timing, registration, and so forth on the day of the event. The meet director will tell you exactly what needs to be done. If you're going to take part in the event also, you'll typically do that at the very beginning (before the regular competition) or at the end. Absolutely no experience is required, and it's very easy, but these people are essential!

So, now that you what's involved, why not scan over the list of events this year and find one or two nearby that you can help with as meet staff, or even better, as meet director or course setter. Then call Bill Cusworth (322-6693) for Cascade events, or the listed club president or event contact for other events. If you're still unsure of what's involved, call us anyway, and we'll explain it to you. For future events, give us your name now, and we'll call you as the event approaches. Thanks for your help!

Our wondrous outreach coordinator, Rick Hood, made connections with the Puget Sound Mycological Society (mushroom club) and arranged for a talk to be presented and an honorarium to be provided. As all might have guessed, the talk was to be on 'finding your way in the woods' as obviously that is what we orienteers are reasonably experienced at. The Cascade member with both the interest and presentation ability turned out to be Anne York. Anne presented a talk at a regular PSMS club meeting for a mixed age group of mostly adults entitled "An Introduction to Orienteering for Mushroomers or How not to get lost while looking at the ground". She prepared overhead transparencies of sample maps, a compass, what skills one could use relating the two, etc. that were quite helpful. One of these overheads was How orienteering skills can help you: Basic skill to get back to the car Go further into the forest with confidence. Locate new places for mushrooming Relocate a good patch. Relocate an injured partner. She kept the talk humorous without losing the interest of the audience when explaining the serious methods orienteers use that could be applied by mushroomers. Naturally, she also tried to convince them to practice or learn their newly discovered techniques by coming to our local O' meets. Not only was she thanked for the presentation, but several folks gave her their name and address to get more info about O' and at least one came to a meet at Bridle Trails the weekend after the talk. PSMS provided an honorarium of $50 which Anne generously donated to Cascade O. C.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United States Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
